name: Tomato
description: A versatile warm-season crop that produces juicy, flavorful fruits perfect for salads, sauces, and sandwiches.
steps: Start seeds indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost date.
Plant seeds 0.25 inch deep in seed-starting mix.
Keep soil warm (70-80°F) and moist until germination.
Transplant outdoors when soil reaches 60°F and frost danger passes.
Space plants 24-36 inches apart in rows 36 inches apart.
Water deeply at the base 2-3 times per week.
Stake or cage plants when they reach 12 inches tall.
Harvest when fruits are firm and fully colored.

name: Basil
description: An aromatic herb essential in Italian and Thai cuisine, easy to grow and wonderfully fragrant.
steps: Sow seeds indoors 6 weeks before last frost.
Plant 0.25 inch deep and keep soil warm and moist.
Transplant after last frost, spacing 12-18 inches apart.
Pinch off flower buds to encourage bushy leaf growth.
Water regularly, keeping soil consistently moist.
Harvest leaves from the top down once plant is 6 inches tall.

name: Carrot
description: A sweet, crunchy root vegetable that thrives in loose, sandy soil. Great for beginners.
steps: Sow seeds directly outdoors 2-3 weeks before last frost.
Plant 0.25 inch deep in loose, rock-free soil.
Keep soil consistently moist during germination.
Thin seedlings to 2-3 inches apart when 2 inches tall.
Mulch around plants to retain moisture.
Harvest when tops of roots are 0.75 inch in diameter.

name: Lettuce
description: A fast-growing leafy green, perfect for salads. Thrives in cool weather.
steps: Sow seeds 0.25 inch deep in cool soil.
Keep soil moist; lettuce seeds need light to germinate.
Thin to 6-12 inches apart depending on variety.
Water consistently; lettuce is shallow-rooted.
Harvest outer leaves or cut the whole head at soil level.

name: Bell Pepper
description: Colorful, crunchy sweet peppers that love warm weather and produce abundantly.
steps: Start seeds indoors 8-10 weeks before last frost.
Plant 0.25 inch deep; keep at 70-80°F for germination.
Harden off seedlings before transplanting outdoors.
Space 18-24 inches apart in warm, sunny location.
Water deeply but less frequently once established.
Harvest when peppers reach full size and desired color.

name: Strawberry
description: Sweet, juicy berries that can be grown in gardens, containers, or hanging baskets.
steps: Plant crowns in early spring after last frost.
Set crowns so the midpoint is at soil level.
Space 12-18 inches apart in rows 36 inches apart.
Mulch with straw to retain moisture and keep berries clean.
Remove runners in the first year to strengthen plants.
Harvest when berries are fully red and fragrant.

name: Cucumber
description: A refreshing warm-season vine crop, great for fresh eating and pickling.
steps: Sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is 60°F+.
Space hills 36-60 inches apart with 2-3 seeds per hill.
Provide a trellis for climbing varieties.
Water deeply and consistently; cucumbers are 95% water.
Harvest when firm and green, before seeds enlarge.

name: Sunflower
description: A cheerful, towering flower that attracts pollinators and produces edible seeds.
steps: Sow seeds 1 inch deep directly outdoors after last frost.
Space 6-24 inches apart depending on variety size.
Water weekly, deeply at the base.
Stake tall varieties when they reach 3 feet.
Harvest seeds when the back of the head turns brown.

name: Mint
description: A vigorous, aromatic herb perfect for teas, cocktails, and cooking. Grows aggressively.
steps: Best started from cuttings or transplants.
Plant in containers to prevent aggressive spreading.
Keep soil consistently moist.
Harvest leaves regularly to encourage bushy growth.
Cut back to ground level in fall for winter dormancy.

name: Rosemary
description: A fragrant, woody Mediterranean herb excellent for roasting meats and baking.
steps: Start from cuttings for best results.
Plant in well-drained soil with good air circulation.
Water sparingly; rosemary prefers dry conditions.
Prune after flowering to maintain shape.
Protect from harsh winter cold in northern zones.
Harvest sprigs anytime once plant is established.

name: Zucchini
description: A prolific summer squash that produces abundantly with minimal effort.
steps: Sow 1 inch deep after last frost in warm soil.
Space plants 36 inches apart.
Water deeply at the base, avoiding leaves.
Harvest at 6-8 inches for best flavor.
Check daily; zucchini grows very fast.

name: Lavender
description: A beautiful, fragrant perennial with purple blooms that attracts bees and butterflies.
steps: Start seeds indoors 10-12 weeks before last frost.
Press seeds onto surface; they need light to germinate.
Transplant to sunny spot with excellent drainage.
Space 12-18 inches apart.
Water sparingly once established; drought-tolerant.
Prune after flowering to maintain shape.
Harvest flower stems when buds begin to open.

name: Potato
description: A hearty, filling root crop that's easy to grow and stores well for months.
steps: Cut seed potatoes into pieces with 2-3 eyes each.
Let cut pieces cure for 1-2 days before planting.
Plant 4 inches deep, 12 inches apart in trenches.
Hill soil around stems when plants are 6 inches tall.
Continue hilling every 2-3 weeks.
Water consistently but avoid waterlogged soil.
Harvest new potatoes 2-3 weeks after flowering.
Harvest main crop when foliage dies back.

name: Marigold
description: Bright, cheerful flowers that repel pests and make excellent companion plants.
steps: Sow seeds 0.25 inch deep after last frost.
Seeds germinate quickly in warm soil.
Space 8-12 inches apart.
Deadhead spent flowers to promote continuous blooming.
Water at the base to avoid leaf diseases.

name: Spinach
description: A nutritious cool-season green rich in iron and vitamins, perfect for salads and cooking.
steps: Sow 0.5 inch deep in cool soil, 4-6 weeks before last frost.
Space seeds 2 inches apart, thin to 4-6 inches.
Keep soil consistently moist.
Harvest outer leaves when 3-4 inches long.
Plant succession crops every 2 weeks for continuous harvest.
Watch for bolting in warm weather.

name: Garlic
description: A pungent, flavorful bulb used worldwide. Plant in fall for a summer harvest.
steps: Separate cloves from the bulb, keeping papery skin on.
Plant cloves 2 inches deep, pointed end up, 6 inches apart.
Plant in fall, 4-6 weeks before ground freezes.
Mulch heavily with straw for winter protection.
Remove flower stalks (scapes) in spring to focus energy on bulbs.
Stop watering 2 weeks before harvest.
Harvest when lower leaves turn brown.
Cure bulbs in a dry, airy space for 2-4 weeks.

name: Blueberry
description: Sweet, antioxidant-rich berries that grow on attractive bushes for years.
steps: Purchase 2-3 year old bushes for faster fruiting.
Dig hole twice the root ball width.
Amend soil with peat moss or sulfur for acidity.
Space bushes 4-6 feet apart.
Mulch with pine needles or wood chips.
Water consistently; blueberries need even moisture.
Net bushes to protect from birds.
Prune dead wood in late winter.

name: Cilantro
description: A fresh, citrusy herb used in Mexican, Asian, and Indian cuisines. Bolts quickly in heat.
steps: Sow seeds 0.25 inch deep directly outdoors.
Space 6-8 inches apart in a partially shaded spot.
Keep soil moist but not waterlogged.
Plant every 2-3 weeks for continuous harvest.
Harvest leaves when plants are 6 inches tall.
Let some plants bolt for coriander seeds.

name: Pea
description: Sweet, crisp pods that love cool weather. Great for trellising and kids' gardens.
steps: Sow 1 inch deep directly outdoors in early spring.
Space 2-4 inches apart along a trellis or fence.
Provide support for climbing varieties.
Water regularly but avoid soggy soil.
Harvest snap peas when pods are plump.
Pick frequently to encourage more production.

name: Rose
description: The classic garden flower, prized for beauty and fragrance. Requires attentive care.
steps: Purchase bare-root or container roses from a nursery.
Dig a hole 18 inches wide and deep.
Create a soil mound in the center of the hole.
Spread roots over the mound and backfill with amended soil.
Water deeply after planting.
Mulch 2-3 inches around the base.
Prune dead or crossing canes in early spring.
Feed with rose fertilizer monthly during growing season.
Deadhead spent blooms to encourage repeat flowering.
